candidate_diagnosis_assessment.retroperitoneal_hemorrhage: MODERATE evidence - Several cases (PMC5066018-lumbar vein rupture, PMC8872004-iliacus hematoma) demonstrate retroperitoneal bleeding with hemodynamic collapse. However, retroperitoneal bleeding typically does NOT cause tamponade-like physiology with elevated venous pressures without pericardial involvement. The hemodynamic pattern of elevated venous pressures suggests either pericardial constraint or right heart compression, which is atypical for isolated retroperitoneal hemorrhage.
candidate_diagnosis_assessment.access_site_bleeding: RULED OUT - The case explicitly states 'NO access-site bleeding on femoral angiography'. Several cases of femoral pseudoaneurysm (PMC3505917, PMC3971853, PMC6501628) show access-site complications can cause hemodynamic collapse, but femoral angiography was negative in this case.
candidate_diagnosis_assessment.vascular_pseudoaneurysm: MODERATE evidence - Multiple cases demonstrate pseudoaneurysms causing hemodynamic collapse (PMC6195931-uterine, PMC3505917-femoral, PMC8776401-mesenteric). However, these typically present with palpable masses or delayed bleeding. In this PCI case, the extensive dissection with overlapping stents suggests a coronary/vascular etiology, but femoral angiography ruled out access site pseudoaneurysm.

domain_conclusion: The vascular domain analysis identified 23 relevant cases of vascular rupture, pseudoaneurysm, or post-procedural bleeding causing hemodynamic collapse. While vascular bleeding can explain the hemoglobin drop (13.2→11 g/dL) and hypotension (60/40 mmHg), it CANNOT adequately explain the tamponade-like physiology with elevated venous pressures in the ABSENCE of pericardial effusion. Vascular causes (retroperitoneal bleed, access site bleeding, pseudoaneurysm) typically cause hypovolemic shock with LOW venous pressures, not elevated venous pressures. The combination of refractory hypotension, IABP requirement, elevated venous pressures, and no pericardial effusion suggests a process causing right heart compression or obstructive physiology not explained by isolated vascular bleeding. The negative femoral angiography definitively rules out access-site complications.
